Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF ROBERT EDWARD LEE
rulings.Calendar Matter: Demurrer Of Denee Brewster To Petition Of Valorie Gordon For Return Of Property, Etc. Filed On April 21, 2023
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 2] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the court is Demurrer of Denee Brewster to Petition of Valorie Gordon. The demurrer is directed against Petition for Return of Property; Conversion; Breach of Fiduciary Duty; Constructive Fraud; Intentional Interference with Expected Inheritance; For An Accounting; Double Damages; And Attorney's Fees, filed on April 21, 2023. Demurrer of Denee Brewster to Petition of Valorie Gordon is OVERRULED. Per the moving papers, the demurrer is brought as a general demurrer on the grounds of Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10 ("pleading does not state facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action") (see document titled "Demurrer of Denee Brewster to Petition of Valorie Gordon for Return of Property, et. Filed on April 21, 2023" at 1:26-27). A demurrer is a pleading used to challenge the legal sufficiency of an opponent's pleading based on defects that appear either on the face of the pleading under attack or from matters outside the pleading that are judicially noticeable. (See Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal. 3d 311, 318; County of Fresno v. Shelton (1998) 66 Cal. App. 4th 996, 1008, 1009.) When considering a demurrer, the trial court must accept as true all material facts pleaded in the complaint and those arising by reasonable implication. (Moore v. Conliffe (1994) 7 Cal. 4th 634, 638; Montclair Parkowners Ass'n v. City of Montclair (1999) 76 Cal. App. 4th 784.) This does not apply to allegations expressing mere conclusions of law, or allegations contradicted by the exhibits to the complaint or by matters of which judicial notice may be taken. (Vance v. Villa Park Mobilehome Estates (1995) 36 Cal. App. 4th 698, 709.) A complaint may be read as if it included matters judicially noticed, and such matters may show the complaint fails to state a cause of action, even though its bare allegations do not disclose the defect. (Code Civil Procedure section 430.30(a); City of Atascadero v. Merrill Lynch, Pierce, Fenner & Smith, Inc. (1998) 68 Cal. App. 4th 445, 459; ABF Capital Corp. v. Berglass (2005) 130 Cal. App. 4th 825.) Four Star Electric, Inc. v. F & H Construction (1992) 7 Cal. App. 4th 1375, 1379; Ricard v. Grobstein, Goldman, Stevenson, Siegel, LeVine & Mangel (1992) 6 Cal. App. 4th 157, 160.) Written instruments which are the foundations of the causes of action and attached to the complaint as exhibits may also be examined by the court. (See SCEcorp v. Sup. Ct. (1992) 3 Cal. App. 4th 673, 677.) A demurrer must demonstrate no right to any relief under the cause of action. (See, Augustine v. Trucco (1954) 124 Cal.App.2d 229, 236.) "[A] demurrer which attacks an entire pleading should be overruled if one of the counts therein is not vulnerable to the objection [citation.] If any count or cause of action in a complaint setting up several count or causes of action is good as against demurrer, a judgment of dismissal for insufficiency of the complaint cannot be sustained." (Shook v. Pearson (1950) 99 Cal. App. 2d 348, 351). In this matter, there are a host of theories advanced by the Demurrer: an allegedly expired statute of limitations on the Petition's claims; a creditor's claim filed in a related probate estate; proceedings in a related probate estate; and laches. A supplemental declaration in support of the demurrer raises issues about an alleged failure to properly serve the petition, as well as the "unfairness" of not granting the demurrer (among other things). None of the claimed bases for the demurrer can be found on the face of the Petition. Many facts and assertions are drawn from outside the Petition and its exhibits. It would be improper for the Court to sustain a demurrer on this presentation. [Part 1 of 2]
